content:
First note that if \(\gcd(a,391)=1\) then
\[
\lcm(a,391)=\tfrac{391\,a}{1}=391a,
\]
while always \(\lcm(a,b)\le a\,b\).  Hence to have
\(\lcm(a,b)>\lcm(a,391)=391a\) one would need \(a\,b>391a\), i.e.\(b>391\).  Thus the only chance to get a small \(b\) is to force \(\gcd(a,391)>1\), which can only be \(17\) or \(23\).

Case 1: \(\gcd(a,391)=17\).  The smallest such \(a\) is \(17\), and then
\[
\lcm(17,391)=391,\quad
\lcm(17,b)=\frac{17b}{\gcd(17,b)}.
\]
To exceed \(391\) we need \(\lcm(17,b)>391\).  If \(17\nmid b\), this is \(17b>391\), i.e.\ \(b>23\), so the smallest integer \(b\) is \(24\).

Case 2: \(\gcd(a,391)=23\).  The smallest such \(a\) is \(23\), and
\[
\lcm(23,391)=391,\quad
\lcm(23,b)=\frac{23b}{\gcd(23,b)}.
\]
If \(23\nmid b\) then we need \(23b>391\), i.e.\ \(b>17\), so the smallest \(b\) is \(18\).

No smaller \(b\) can work (one checks directly that for \(b\le17\) there is no \(a<391\) with \(\lcm(a,b)>\lcm(a,391)\)).  Hence the minimum possible \(b\) is

\[
\boxed{18}.
\]
